comments.text: With a computer implementation, the following values have been checked for $c=3/4$: For $100 \le n \le 104$, the maximum size of such a family is respectively $34,37,32,38$ and $35$. So yes, it does depend on $n$ in an irregular way. For $2|n, 3 \nmid n$ and $n$ sufficiently large, it might be that an (maybe the unique) extremal construction consists of all multiples of $3$ and the large elements ($n/2 \le x \le cn$) which are equivalent to $n/2 \mod 3$. For $6 \mid n$ and $n$ not too large, it may well be that an extremal construction is $[\frac n2, \lfloor cn \rfloor ] \cup [1,k]$ for the largest $k$ satisfying $\binom{k+1}{2}+ \lfloor cn \rfloor <n.$ This has size $n/4+O( \sqrt n)$. For $6 \mid n$, $n$ large and $5 \nmid n$ ($n \equiv 4r \pmod 5$), the construction $\{ 1 \le x \le \lfloor cn \rfloor \colon 5 \mid x \vee (x \equiv r \pmod 5 \wedge x>n/4) \vee (x \equiv 2r \pmod 5 \wedge x>n/2) \}$ nevertheless is of size $0.3n + O(1)$ and thus larger. The latter construction also works for any other smallest prime $q$ not dividing $n$, so the exact form seems to depend on both the prime factorization of $n$ and the size of $n$.

The maximum of the above still doesnt give the optimum. E.g. $2\mid n, 3\mid n-2$ and $c=3/4$. Take the multiples of $3$ (bounded by $cn$) and the numbers which are $\equiv 1 \mod 3$ and between $n/2$ and $cn$. Then one has a construction with $n/3$ elements, while the above constructions gave only $n/4$. This construction also generalises to larger smallest prime(power) not dividing $n$, giving some other candidates.

If $c\ge 1$, then the maximum is trivially $(c-1/2)n.$ Take all $m$ for which either $n\le 2m<2n$ or $m>n$. (Sharpness by considering pairs $\{x,n-x\}$) For $1/2<c<1$, the maximum is bounded by $cn/2+2.$ For this consider pairs $\{x,n-x\}$, find 2 elements with sum $(1-c)n-1$ and then $2$ other ls with sum $cn+1.$ Possible constructions: For $1/b\le c<1/(b-1)$, One can take those $n/b \le n <n/(b-1)$ and a few more or $n/(b+1) \le m \le n/b$ + some (a max set such that the sum of b eleements too large and of b-1 is too small and similar). One can also take the smallest prime power $q \nmid n$ and take the multiples of $q$ (and a few more without sumset equal to $n \mod q$). If the maximum would be the maximum among the ones obtained from size-constrant and modulo-constraint, similar to Erdos matching conjecture, it indeed depends on $n$ in a (periodic) irregular way.

If $p$ is the smallest prime that does not divide $n$, then you can take $A$ to be the multiples of $p$ so $|A|=\lfloor \frac{\lfloor cn\rfloor}{p}\rfloor $. Let $c\leq 1$. For an integer $k\geq 1$, the set $A=[\frac{n}{k+1},\frac{n}{k}]\cap\mathbb{N}$ always satisfies the condition. So letting $t=\lceil \frac{1}{c}\rceil$, we have $|A|\geq \max\{(c-\frac{1}{t})n, \frac{n}{t(t+1)}\}$.
